Why Minor Sounds Sad

The series so far has covered harmonics, roughness curves, tuning, and scales. Now it adds stacks of three notes. Take a scale from part four, stand on any note, and grab every other note as you climb: 1–3–5. That stack is a triad — a chord — and many listeners describe one kind as bright and another as sad. Parts one and two help explain some of that difference.

Step 1 · The chord microscopeMajor and minor

Put chords under the same microscope we used for intervals: every note carries its harmonic ladder, and the chord's character lives in how the ladders interleave. Click around the lab below — roots on the piano, qualities on the buttons — and watch the collision row at the top.

The major triad's frequencies sit in the ratio 4:5:6 — three consecutive rungs of a single harmonic series. A major chord on C is, acoustically, a fragment of the harmonic recipe of one giant C two octaves down; part one showed your ear spends its whole life fusing exactly such patterns into single notes. So major doesn't just sound pleasant — it sounds settled, like one complex object. The white flares in the figure show dense harmonic alignment between the voices.

The minor triad contains the same two intervals — a major third and a minor third — stacked in the other order. The fifth still locks (10:15 = 2:3), but as a whole the chord anchors only into the much weaker 10:12:15 lattice: it implies no single fundamental your ear can rally around. Nothing clashes — minor is consonant — but the fusion is fainter and more ambiguous. Cultural convention strongly shapes the emotional meaning of minor, while the weaker harmonic fusion is one acoustic difference between the two chords.

Then compare diminished (two minor thirds, with a tritone instead of a perfect fifth) and augmented (two major thirds, perfectly symmetric, so no note clearly establishes the root). Chord quality follows from how the two thirds are arranged.

Deep dive: inversions, and why the root survives them

Play C–E–G, then move the C up an octave: E–G–C. Different lowest note, same chord — listeners still hear "C major, slightly rearranged". The reason is the microscope again: the pattern of alignments between the ladders barely changes under octave shifts (doubling a frequency slides its ladder onto itself), so the ear's best-fundamental inference still answers "C". Harmony theory leans hard on this: a chord is an equivalence class of voicings, which is precisely what lets the next demo's voices wander freely without changing the song.

Aside · Rameau and the corps sonore

Jean-Philippe Rameau's Treatise on Harmony (1722) — the book that made "music theory" a discipline — argued that all harmony grows from the resonating body (corps sonore) and its overtones: the major triad is audibly sitting inside every single musical tone. He then spent decades publicly wrestling with minor, trying scheme after scheme to derive it as naturally (undertones? co-vibration?). None quite worked. Modern accounts retain his emphasis on overtones while treating minor as a different arrangement of the same interval material.

Step 2 · Chords in timeTension and resolution

A single chord is a snapshot. Music is what happens when chords move — and they don't move randomly. Build a triad on each degree of the C-major scale and you get the seven citizens of the key: three major (I, IV, V), three minor (ii, iii, vi), one diminished (vii°). Songs treat them not as equals but as roles: I is home; IV leans away; and V — the chord built on the fifth — strongly points back toward home.

The pull is mechanical, and you have already met both of its parts. Add the seventh to V (making V7 — in C major: G–B–D–F) and the chord traps a tritone between B and F, part two's roughest interval, an instability demanding motion. And the resolution is no mystery either: B sits one half step under C, F one half step over E — the two most loaded positions in part four's scale. Resolving to I moves each tense note by the smallest possible interval.

Watch the threads while it plays. The bass strides from root to root, but the three upper voices are lazy: at each chord change every voice slides to the nearest available chord tone, often not moving at all. That laziness — voice leading — is why a chord change sounds like transformation rather than replacement, and the demo computes it the way a chorale writer would: try the assignments, keep the cheapest. Watch the amber tension lane rise on V and fall on I. Then press deceptive: same wind-up, but V resolves to vi — the relative minor. Composers use that deceptive resolution to delay the expected ending.

Aside · Four chords, several thousand songs

The loop below — I–V–vi–IV — underlies a comedy-routine-length list of hits ("Let It Be", "No Woman No Cry", "Africa", "Let Her Go", and the Axis of Awesome's forty-song medley). Its trick is built into the tension lane: the strongest release (V→I) never happens inside the loop — V falls deceptively to vi instead — so the cycle hands out motion without ever spending it, and the song can spin indefinitely. The 12-bar blues, ii–V–I jazz turnarounds, and the minor anthem loop in the preset list are the same physics with different spending habits.

Deep dive: the lazy-voice algorithm

At each chord change the demo enumerates all six assignments of the three voices to the new chord's three pitch classes, places each voice at the assigned class's nearest octave (clamped to a singable band), and scores each assignment by total semitones moved, with a penalty for voices landing in unison. Lowest score wins. That tiny search reproduces a surprising amount of 18th-century part-writing doctrine — common tones held, steps preferred over leaps — not because the rules were arbitrary taste, but because the rules were always a compressed description of laziness.

EpilogueThe harmonic system so far

Count what the series now holds. Notes with recipes (part one). Intervals ranked by harmonic alignment (part two). A twelve-note grid (part three). A seven-note landscape with a home (part four). And now chords — objects with quality (geometry of thirds) and function (distance from home, with V7 as the spring) — moving through time on lazy voices. Those are central parts of Western tonal music. What the system does not yet produce is a song: nothing in our machinery decides when notes happen, or which melody rides on top of the chords.

The finale adds algorithms for rhythm and melody, then lets the browser generate complete arrangements.

Concluded in Part six: The Infinite Jukebox — Euclidean rhythm, random-walk melody, a generative band built from every post in the series, and visuals to get lost in.

Sound by Tone.js. Standing on the shoulders of: Rameau (1722) · Helmholtz (1863) · the Axis of Awesome (2009)
